item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The author doesn't have to listen to criticism. If they like the article the way it is, then it's ok

As a general note, given that the author is writing for a voting audience, while it's technically true that the author can just ignore feedback it's not really something that encourages wanting to help the author improve their work. Furthermore, if multiple readers state the same issue is preventing them from upvoting, it's in the author's interest to address the issue.

I just wanted to point out that those "faults" you mention are really nothing more than subjective disagreements with the author's vision.

I'm not entirely sure this is the case here, since the provided example is somewhat wooden dialogue difficult to take seriously that breaks up a fluent read. I'm not sure if this is the author's intention?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author doesn't have to listen to criticism. If they like the article the way it is, then it's ok. I don't find trying to change every little thing in a skip to please everyone to be a worthy or even dignified thing to do for a writer, specially if they have sound or stylistic reasons for every detail in their text. I'm sorry if it sounds like I'm antagonizing you, but I just wanted to point out that those "faults" you mention are really nothing more than subjective disagreements with the author's vision.

item.title: Licensing Junior Staff Post - Info
item.content:encoded: As no compliant source for the image used in this article could be found it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is all kinds of awesome. And every single paragraph it gets better. Including the review ones!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to agree with everyone who said that the premise or the idea of this SCP is amazing and has a lot of potential, but the issues with tone and execution prevented an upvote from me.

These lines from the interview log gave me anomalous cringe.

CC: Me too! I hope it doesn’t get too confusing. [Laughs]
…
CC: Oh, Kat.
…
CC: Wow! I heard they went the extra mile but that’s amazing!

It seems like the author simply agreed to disagree with all the criticisms and did not do anything to rectify the faults in this article, though, so I expect this will stay in the low +s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is great but poor execution. The interview bugged me until I noticed it was conducted by a field agent, presumably undercover: a note clarifying would be nice. But there's a lot of cleanup to do, too. For instance:

Any identified instances of SCP-2809-1 are to be administered amnestics sufficient to remove any memories associated with SCP-2809.

The part I struck through part could just be deleted.

Also, since it's implied that the Foundation has yet to intercept an instance of SCP-2809-1 before the trip, a lot of the detail regarding how they're picked up etc. should probably be omitted. Maybe a note can be given about them not appearing on any airport security cameras.
The story told in this skip is reminiscent of The Twilight Zone episode, Walking Distance. It really resonates with me for personal reasons. Nice job, author!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gentlefolks,

I can't argue with you.

The names of the reviewers were my idea of a little joke. I can see how they could be jarring if they jump out at you in the wrong way.

I guess I focused on the emotional side of the piece more than I did making sure that it was narratively satisfying. For me it was more about what it feels like for the -1s than about a time-travel mystery.

Genuinely sorry you didn't enjoy it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that's about where it clicked off for me too.

It feels like… It feels like there are a whole lot of elements in here that are intentionally downplayed. They go away in an airport bus, the -1s don't think anything is abnormal, there's no evidence that anything bad happened in the past (except for a pop-culture reference on a damn travel website probably suggesting the grandfather paradox, the most entry-level time travel paradox). They can't track the server, they can't find the company, and they can't really do much of anything else about it, which are all story dead-ends.

(I'm ok with the interviewer acting like a reporter, that only makes sense.)

There's nothing about this that makes it jump out and feel special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, but the last one coming from "WyldStallion" is really on-the-nose and jarring, as referential jokes go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the SCP but I think the tone of the interview doesn't really work with the rest of the piece. I think that making the Foundation interviewer act more professionally might help. I liked the logs, and the premise is interesting, so neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shaggydredlocks,

What's become rather a run-on response, sorry
Hide

I see exactly where you're coming from. The interview just isn't going to work for everyone. I'm sorry you're in that camp 'cause you've always given good feedback in the past but I'm leaving it as it is.

I was trying to show how the experience had helped the interviewee to process the death of a parent. She was still sad about it but had managed to come to terms with it in a way many people never do.

I put the "do not seem to find anything unusual" bit below the interview because I wanted a cause a little bit of cognitive dissonance in the reader while they were working through the interview.

I had hoped that the interview would provide a little sweet emotional impact with just a hint of bitterness for flavour but I guess it didn't do it for you. I'm sorry about that.

Still - I appreciate the feedback and I'm glad you liked the reviews. Thanks, person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hi im stel,

Thanks for that. All good points. Followed your suggestions almost word for word.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hi all!

Thanks for getting this far. Thanks also to psul and Tuomey Tombstone in the forums. The majority of any problems you encounter would be from me not listening to these guys enough.

Thanks should also go to the good folks of the wiki for building a place and a community for my ramblings to settle.

OK - enough back-slapping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked the interview; it showed a Foundation staffer treating an interviewee like a human being, which was charmingly different. The idea of meeting your own parents decades before you were born - huh. I found myself wondering what Quondam* is getting out of this. Enjoyable.
+1

*I'm embarrassed at how long it took me to get the joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview really killed this for me.

For starters, I was expecting something more poignant or somber, given the subject matter, so for it to be lighthearted and casual was jarring. I think I'd have been eased into that if you were to move the rest of the description above the log ('do not seem to find anything unusual about this' in particular would have been handy to know beforehand). Second, there's nothing particularly noteworthy in it. It's for the most part a rundown of of a bland process.

I think it'd be better off without it, the reviews were the real highlight of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but what throws the article off for me is a handfull of wonky or run on sentences.
Some examples:

+ crit
- crit

At some point in an approximately one month period immediately after an instance of SCP-2809-1’s parent or parents die,

Shouldn't "die" be past tense here?

If an instance of SCP-2809-1 clicks on an instance of SCP-2809-2 they are taken to the website of Quondam Holidays. Quondam Holidays is designated as SCP-2809-3.

I feel like this could be shortened into one sentence, as "Quondam Holidays" repeated twice sort of seems odd to me.

Investigation into Quondam Holidays is ongoing but little information has been discovered about them at this time apart from that gathered from interviews with SCP-2809-1 instances and their reviews of Quondam's service.

A bit of a run on here. Maybe something to make it less wordy, such as

Investigation into SCP-2809-3 is ongoing, with most information about them gathered from interviews of SCP-2809-1 instances and online reviews of SCP-2809-3.
